markdown:
## Two-Hop Architecture Chain: GPT-3 → GPT-2 → Attention Is All You Need

### Hop 1: GPT-3 inherits GPT-2's architecture

In §2.1 ("Model and Architectures"), the GPT-3 paper states that it adopts GPT-2's model and architecture wholesale, with one targeted modification (alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention):

> "We use the same model and architecture as GPT-2 [RWC+19], including the modified initialization, pre-normalization, and reversible tokenization described therein, with the exception that we use alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention patterns in the layers of the transformer, similar to the Sparse Transformer [CGRS19]." [1]

What this hop establishes: GPT-3 does not define its own core architecture; it explicitly reuses the GPT-2 model and inherits its initialization, pre-normalization, and tokenization. GPT-3's architectural novelty is restricted to the attention-pattern swap to the Sparse Transformer variant, while the underlying "transformer" architecture is carried over from GPT-2.

### Hop 2: GPT-2 bases its architecture on the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017)

In §2.3 ("Model"), the GPT-2 paper declares its architecture directly:

> "We use a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) based architecture for our LMs. The model largely follows the details of the OpenAI GPT model (Radford et al., 2018) with a few modifications." [2]

What this hop establishes: GPT-2 anchors its model to the Transformer paper by Vaswani et al., 2017, then layers modifications (pre-activation layer normalization, modified residual-path initialization, expanded vocabulary, larger 1024-token context) on top of it. The Transformer is the architectural foundation GPT-2 builds on, with the OpenAI GPT model providing the closer template.

### The origin paper

"Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017) introduces the Transformer in its own words as:

> "In this work we propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output." [3]

The model architecture (§3) is an encoder–decoder structure with stacked self-attention and point-wise, fully connected layers: "The Transformer follows this overall architecture using stacked self-attention and point-wise, fully connected layers for both the encoder and decoder." [4]

### Chain summary

| Hop | From → To | Relationship established | Citation locus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GPT-3 → GPT-2 | GPT-3 reuses GPT-2's architecture verbatim (except for sparse attention) | GPT-3 §2.1 [1] |
| 2 | GPT-2 → Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) | GPT-2 is a "Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) based architecture" with GPT-style modifications | GPT-2 §2.3 [2] |

### Note on what is *not* claimed

GPT-3 does not cite "Attention Is All You Need" directly as the source of its architecture. The connection between GPT-3 and the Transformer is mediated entirely through GPT-2's own §2.3 citation; no direct GPT-3-to-Transformer architectural citation is invented here.

Sources
[1] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 2 Approach, p. 8
[2] Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners, 2.2. Input Representation, p. 4
[3] Attention Is All You Need, 1 Introduction, p. 2
[4] Attention Is All You Need, 3 Model Architecture, p. 3
